Mental Health Trends in England: An Epidemiological Analysis

Usman Adamu

2026-05-11

Introduction

Mental health disorders represent one of the leading causes of disability and disease burden in England. Common mental health conditions including depression and anxiety affect millions of people annually, with significant implications for individuals, families, and the NHS.

This report presents an epidemiological analysis of mental health trends in England using data from the Office for Health Inequalities and Disparities (OHID) Fingertips platform. The analysis examines depression prevalence trends over time, geographic variation across local authorities, and the burden of mental health related hospital admissions.

Research Questions

  1. How has depression prevalence changed in England between 2012 and 2024?
  2. Which local authorities have the highest and lowest depression prevalence?
  3. What is the geographic distribution of depression across England?
  4. How have mental health hospital admissions changed over time?

Data and Methods

Data Source

Data were obtained from the OHID Fingertips platform using the fingertipsR package in R. Four indicators were extracted at local authority level (Counties & Unitary Authorities from April 2023):

  • Depression QOF prevalence (Indicator 848)
  • Depression new diagnosis (Indicator 90646)
  • % reporting depression or anxiety (Indicator 93376)
  • Mental health hospital admissions (Indicator 90812)

Packages Used

# FIX: Added echo=TRUE so package list is visible in rendered document
library(fingertipsR)
library(tidyverse)
library(tmap)
library(sf)

Data Loading

Data Cleaning

The datasets were checked for missing values and filtered to retain only local authority level data, excluding aggregate regional and national figures where appropriate.

## Missing values in depression data: 0
## Missing values in admissions data: 0

Results

1. National Depression Prevalence Trend 2012-2024

The chart below shows how depression prevalence has changed across England over the past decade.

Depression prevalence in England increased steadily from 5.8% in 2012/13 to 14.3% in 2024/25 — representing a 146% increase over 12 years. This rising trend is consistent with national evidence showing that depression prevalence has increased continuously across England since the early 2010s 1.

Multiple intersecting factors have been identified in the literature as drivers of this increase. Socioeconomic deprivation is one of the strongest predictors of depression, with evidence showing it explained up to 39% of the variance in depression prevalence across English localities in 2020 1. Social isolation and loneliness — both established risk factors for depression — have increased markedly in England over the past decade 3. The cost of living crisis has further compounded these pressures, with ONS data linking financial stress directly to depressive symptoms 6. Additionally, improved detection and diagnosis through primary care, alongside growing mental health literacy, may have contributed to higher recorded prevalence independent of true increases in burden 1.

A slight flattening was observed between 2020/21 and 2021/22. This likely reflects reduced GP recording capacity during the COVID-19 pandemic rather than a true reduction in prevalence. Pierce et al. 4 demonstrated that depression rates in the UK increased sharply during the first lockdown, driven by loneliness, bereavement, and economic uncertainty. Post-pandemic, the upward trend resumed as services recovered and pent-up demand emerged. Confidence intervals were extremely narrow throughout, reflecting the large population denominator and high precision of the national estimate.

Formal statistical testing confirmed that the upward trend in depression prevalence was highly significant. Linear regression of prevalence against time identified an average increase of 0.77 percentage points per year across the study period (β = 0.77, p < 0.001, R² = 0.995). The exceptionally high R-squared value indicates that the trend is remarkably consistent and linear over time, with time alone explaining 99.5% of the variance in national depression prevalence. This sustained and statistically robust increase underscores the scale of the growing depression burden in England and the urgency of population-level public health responses targeting the upstream determinants of mental health.1,2

2. Local Authority Variation in Depression Prevalence

The charts below show the local authorities with the highest and lowest depression prevalence in the most recent data year (2024/25).

Considerable geographic variation in depression prevalence exists across English local authorities, with a clear North-South gradient apparent in the data. A landmark 12-year longitudinal spatial study by Daras et al.  1, conducted in collaboration with the University of Liverpool and NIHR Applied Research Collaboration North West Coast, identified the North West of England as having the highest concentration of depression hotspots in the country, with 43% of areas in the region showing a high rate of increase in prevalence between 2011 and 2022. The North East similarly showed persistently high rates, with one in three residents living in areas marked by sustained high depression prevalence 1.

By contrast, London had the lowest percentage of depression hotspots, with only 0.38% of its area falling into the highest prevalence category — a pattern mirrored across much of the South East. The authors identified neighbourhood socioeconomic deprivation as a key driver of this geographic inequality, explaining between 3% and 39% of variance in depression prevalence across localities. These findings are consistent with wider evidence that lower socioeconomic status — encompassing income, employment, education and housing — is one of the strongest predictors of depression risk 2. The pattern observed in the current analysis is therefore consistent with established health inequality evidence and highlights areas requiring targeted public health intervention.

3. Geographic Distribution of Depression Prevalence

The map below shows depression prevalence across English local authorities in 2024/25, revealing clear geographic patterns.

The choropleth map reveals a pronounced North-South gradient in depression prevalence across English local authorities in 2024/25, with northern areas consistently showing darker shading indicating higher prevalence. This pattern is well-established in the literature. Daras et al. 1, in the first nationwide longitudinal spatial analysis of depression in England, identified the North West as having the highest concentration of depression hotspots nationally — with 17.4% of the region’s area showing persistently high values surrounded by similarly high neighbouring areas, and 43% of areas experiencing a high rate of increase between 2011 and 2022. The North East showed similar patterns, with one in three residents living in areas of persistently high depression prevalence 1.

By contrast, London and the South East consistently showed the lowest depression prevalence, a finding corroborated by Parliamentary analysis of NHS data which identified the North of England as hosting three of the top five sub-regions for depression prevalence nationally 7. The Health Foundation 8 similarly documented higher rates of diagnosed illness, including depression, in the North of England compared to southern regions, linking these patterns to geographic socioeconomic inequalities and the Index of Multiple Deprivation.

The North-South health divide in England is a long-standing and well-documented phenomenon. Prior to the COVID-19 pandemic, life expectancy at birth was more than two years higher in London and the South East than in the North of England 7, with the Institute of Health Equity documenting widening health inequalities in life expectancy across most English regions since 2010 9. The depression patterns observed in the current analysis are therefore consistent with broader geographic health inequalities driven by structural differences in socioeconomic deprivation, employment, housing quality and access to services.

It should be noted that three local authorities (Poole, Isles of Scilly and City of London) are shown in grey due to the absence of matched data following the geographic boundary join. This reflects a known limitation of linking administrative health data to geographic boundary files, where name mismatches between data sources result in unmatched records.

4. Deprivation and Depression — Is There a Relationship?

The scatter plot below examines the relationship between neighbourhood deprivation and depression prevalence across English local authorities in 2024/25.

## Matched local authorities: 152

Linear regression identified a statistically significant positive association between deprivation score and depression prevalence across English local authorities (β = 0.13, p < 0.001, R² = 0.10). For every one unit increase in IMD deprivation score, depression prevalence increased by approximately 0.13 percentage points, confirming the expected direction of association — more deprived areas have higher recorded depression prevalence.1

However, the relatively modest R-squared value of 10.4% — substantially lower than the 66.7% observed for the deprivation-life expectancy relationship — warrants careful interpretation. Depression QOF prevalence reflects recorded diagnosis in primary care rather than true population prevalence, and areas with greater GP access, higher mental health literacy and better service availability may record more depression independently of underlying deprivation burden.2 This represents a form of ascertainment bias that may attenuate the observed association between deprivation and recorded depression prevalence. Additionally, depression is a multifactorial condition influenced by social isolation, life events, age structure, employment status and service quality — factors not fully captured by a single deprivation index.3 A multivariate model incorporating additional predictors would likely explain a greater proportion of the variance and provide a more complete account of the geographic distribution of depression in England. These findings nonetheless confirm deprivation as a statistically significant predictor of depression prevalence and are consistent with established evidence on the social determinants of mental health.1,3

5. Depression Prevalence Across Deprivation Quintiles

Depression prevalence showed a general positive gradient across deprivation quintiles, with the most deprived local authorities (quintile 5) recording the highest mean depression prevalence at approximately 16.8%, compared to approximately 13.0% in the least deprived quintile — a difference of nearly four percentage points.1 Error bars representing standard error were consistently small across quintiles, indicating low variability within groups and reliable estimates.

Notably, the gradient was not perfectly linear — a slight dip in mean prevalence was observed at quintile 3 before rising again in quintiles 4 and 5. This non-linearity may reflect the known ascertainment bias in QOF depression recording, whereby areas in the middle deprivation range may have better primary care access and higher rates of diagnosis relative to true prevalence than either extreme.2 Alternatively, it may reflect genuine variation in depression burden driven by factors beyond deprivation alone, including social cohesion, employment patterns and community resilience. Regardless of this mid-range variation, the substantially higher depression prevalence in the most deprived quintile compared to the least deprived confirms the social gradient in depression burden and is consistent with established evidence that socioeconomic disadvantage is a significant determinant of mental health outcomes in England.1,3

6. Mental Health Hospital Admissions Trend

The chart below shows how mental health related hospital admissions have changed over time across English local authorities.

Mental health hospital admissions among under-18s in England increased substantially over the study period, rising from approximately 85 per 100,000 in 2010/11 to a peak in 2021/22 before a partial decline. A national cohort study by Ward et al. 11, published in The Lancet Child & Adolescent Health, found that admissions for mental health concerns among children aged 5–18 years in England increased by 65% between 2012/13 and 2021/22 — a rise substantially greater than the 10% increase in all-cause admissions over the same period. Self-harm accounted for 53% of all mental health admissions in 2021/22, while eating disorders showed a striking 514% increase over the decade 11.

The COVID-19 pandemic acted as a significant accelerant of pre-existing trends. Evidence from the Clinical Practice Research Datalink showed increased incidence of eating disorders and self-harm among adolescents in the UK in the two years following pandemic onset, particularly among girls aged 12–17 years 12. The subsequent decline observed from 2022/23 may reflect a partial normalisation of service demand following the acute pandemic period, though admissions remain substantially above pre-pandemic levels, suggesting a sustained increase in the severity of presentations requiring inpatient care. Geographical variation was also marked, with a four-fold difference in admission rates between integrated care board areas in 2021/22 11, highlighting inequitable access to community mental health services across England.

Female under-18s had consistently higher mental health hospital admission rates than males throughout the study period. This pattern is supported by national evidence: Ward et al. 11 found that increases in admissions for mental health concerns in females aged 11–15 years were particularly steep, rising by 112.8% between 2012/13 and 2021/22, with females in this age group accounting for nearly one third of all admissions in 2021/22. Eating disorders — which disproportionately affect young women — showed the most dramatic increase, with hospital admissions rising by 514% over the decade 11. Of the 24,300 hospital admissions for eating disorders in 2020/21, the large majority involved women and girls 15.

A striking divergence emerged during 2020/21 — female admissions increased sharply while male admissions declined. Solmi et al. 12 identified increased presentations for self-harm and eating disorders among girls aged 12–17 years in the UK in 2021, whilst presentation rates for boys in the same age group showed no equivalent increase. The disproportionate mental health burden of COVID-19 lockdowns on young women has been attributed to multiple factors, including increased domestic violence exposure, intensified social media use amplifying body image concerns, loss of educational and social structures, and the unique psychological pressures of adolescent female development 12.

The concurrent decline in male admissions during 2020/21 is unlikely to reflect improved mental health outcomes. A systematic review by Sheikh et al. 13 identified conformity to masculine norms and self-stigma as consistent barriers to help-seeking among adolescent boys and young men in the UK. The review found that adherence to ideals of self-reliance and emotional stoicism led young men to perceive seeking help as a threat to their masculinity, reducing engagement with mental health services. Smith et al. 14 further identified denial of symptoms, poor rapport with healthcare professionals, and lower mental health literacy as additional barriers specific to men. The “gender paradox in suicide” — where men have higher suicide rates despite women having higher rates of self-harm and help-seeking — underscores the clinical significance of these barriers 16. Both trends warrant urgent policy attention.

Conclusion

This report presents an epidemiological analysis of mental health trends in England using four indicators from the OHID Fingertips platform, covering the period 2010 to 2024. The analysis drew on real NHS data accessed programmatically via the fingertipsR package, and examined depression prevalence trends over time, geographic variation across local authorities, the relationship between deprivation and depression, and mental health hospital admissions with a focus on under-18s and gender disparities.

Key Findings

1. Sustained and statistically significant rise in depression prevalence

Depression QOF prevalence increased by 146% between 2012/13 and 2024/25, rising from 5.8% to 14.3% of the adult population aged 18 and over. Formal statistical testing confirmed this trend was highly significant, with depression prevalence increasing by an average of 0.77 percentage points per year (β = 0.77, p < 0.001, R² = 0.995). This trajectory is consistent with national evidence identifying socioeconomic deprivation, social isolation, cost of living pressures and improved detection as key drivers of recorded prevalence.1,2 A temporary flattening during 2020/21 likely reflects disrupted GP recording capacity during the COVID-19 pandemic rather than a true decline.4

2. Deprivation as a significant predictor of depression

Linear regression confirmed a statistically significant positive association between deprivation score and depression prevalence across English local authorities (β = 0.13, p < 0.001, R² = 0.10). The deprivation quintile analysis further demonstrated a clear social gradient — local authorities in the most deprived quintile recorded mean depression prevalence of approximately 16.8% compared to 13.0% in the least deprived quintile. The modest R-squared value reflects the known ascertainment bias in QOF recording and the multifactorial nature of depression, rather than an absence of association.2,3

3. Marked geographic inequality

Substantial geographic variation in depression prevalence exists across English local authorities, with a clear North-South gradient. The North West and North East of England showed the highest depression prevalence, consistent with established health inequality evidence linking neighbourhood deprivation to depression burden.1,8 London and the South East consistently showed the lowest prevalence.

4. Rising mental health admissions among under-18s

Mental health hospital admissions among under-18s increased substantially across the study period, with self-harm accounting for over half of all admissions and eating disorders showing the most dramatic relative increase. The COVID-19 pandemic accelerated pre-existing trends, particularly among adolescent girls.12

5. Persistent and widening gender disparity in admissions

Female under-18s had consistently higher mental health hospital admission rates than males throughout the study period. During 2020/21 this disparity widened sharply, reflecting both the disproportionate mental health burden of lockdowns on young women and the structural barriers to help-seeking among young men, including stigma and conformity to masculine norms.13,16

Limitations

Several methodological limitations should be acknowledged.

First, QOF depression prevalence reflects recorded diagnosis in primary care rather than true population prevalence. The modest R-squared value in the deprivation-depression regression (R² = 0.10) is consistent with this ascertainment bias — areas with better GP access may record more depression independently of true burden.2

Second, the depression prevalence indicator contains only a “Persons” sex category with no Male/Female breakdown, preventing sex-stratified analysis of prevalence trends. This is a limitation of the indicator design rather than the analytical approach.

Third, the hospital admissions indicator is specific to under-18s and does not capture adult admission trends, which may follow different patterns.

Fourth, this is an ecological analysis. Area-level associations cannot be used to infer individual-level causal relationships.

Fifth, the most recent data point (2024/25) for the national depression trend is a modelled estimate and should be interpreted with appropriate caution.

Recommendations

1. Targeted mental health investment in deprived areas. The clear deprivation gradient — both in the regression analysis and the quintile chart — confirms that universal approaches to depression prevention will be insufficient. Targeted investment in primary care mental health services in the most deprived local authorities, particularly in the North West and North East, is required.1,3

2. Gender-responsive CAMHS services. The marked and growing gender disparity in under-18 admissions — driven particularly by eating disorders and self-harm among adolescent girls — requires dedicated service responses. NHS England’s eating disorder waiting time targets have not been consistently met.11

3. Male-friendly mental health services. The decline in male admissions during the pandemic, combined with evidence of structural barriers to help-seeking in young men, suggests significant unmet need. Gender-sensitive service design and public mental health campaigns targeting male help-seeking are indicated.13,16

4. Strengthening routine surveillance. The absence of sex-stratified depression prevalence data in the QOF indicator highlights gaps in routine surveillance. Investment in nationally representative mental health prevalence surveys is essential for monitoring true population burden.

5. Addressing social determinants upstream. Evidence consistently identifies socioeconomic deprivation, social isolation and financial stress as key drivers of depression.1,2,3 Public health approaches must engage with these structural determinants beyond healthcare alone.

Data and Reproducibility

All data were obtained from the OHID Fingertips platform via the fingertipsR package (v1.1.0) in R. Four indicators were extracted at Counties and Unitary Authorities level (AreaTypeID 502): Depression QOF prevalence (Indicator 848), depression new diagnosis (Indicator 90646), percentage reporting depression or anxiety (Indicator 93376), and mental health hospital admissions (Indicator 90812). Deprivation data used the IMD 2019 indicator (Indicator 93553). All analysis was conducted in R version 4.5.3 using the tidyverse, fingertipsR, tmap and sf packages. The complete R Markdown source code is fully reproducible. Data accessed April 2026.
